cards.back: Any innovation that includes a program as an integral part of its function. The three types are: physical (e.g. self-driving car), nonphysical software (e.g. photo editing app), and nonphysical concept (e.g. e-commerce).

cards.front: How does collaboration improve computing innovations?
cards.back: Multiple perspectives improve design, reflect diverse talents, and help avoid bias. Effective collaboration requires equal participation and voice from all members — it is not the same as group work.

A preference or prejudice embedded in an innovation that disadvantages certain users. Often occurs unintentionally when the development team lacks diverse perspectives. Including diverse collaborators helps identify and avoid it.
Communicating with potential users during development to understand the program's purpose from diverse perspectives. Helps develop a program that fully incorporates those perspectives.
Digital tools that allow programmers to share code, documents, and ideas and provide feedback regardless of location. Examples: version control systems, shared coding platforms, document editors.
A collaboration model where one programmer writes code while another observes and provides real-time feedback. Both roles actively contribute — the observer catches errors the writer might miss.
Communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation. Effective collaborative teams practice all four to ensure equal participation and a shared direction.
To solve problems or to pursue interests through creative expression. Understanding the purpose gives developers an improved ability to build the innovation.
A collection of program statements that performs a specific task when run by a computer. Often referred to as software. A program needs to work for a variety of inputs and situations.
A collection of program statements that is part of a larger program.

How a program functions during execution, often described by how a user interacts with it. A program can be described broadly by what it does, or in detail by both what it does and how its statements accomplish that.
Data sent to a computer for processing by a program. Can come in many forms: tactile, audio, visual, or text. Can come from a user or from other programs. Inputs usually affect the output produced.

An action associated with input data that supplies information to a program. Events can be generated by a key press, mouse click, program start, or any other defined action that affects the flow of execution.
A programming style where program statements execute when triggered by an event rather than through the sequential flow of control.
Any data sent from a program to a device. Can come in many forms: tactile, audio, visual, or text. Usually based on the program's input or prior state.
A program is a complete collection of statements that performs a specific task when run. A code segment is a smaller collection of statements that is part of a larger program.

1. Investigating and reflecting
2. Designing
3. Prototyping
4. Testing
A development process can be ordered and intentional, or exploratory in nature.
A development process that requires refinement and revision based on feedback, testing, or reflection throughout the process. May require revisiting earlier phases.
A development process that breaks the problem into smaller pieces and makes sure each piece works before adding it to the whole.

Iterative: the whole project is continuously refined based on feedback — earlier phases may be revisited at any time.
Incremental: the project is built piece by piece — each piece is completed and verified before the next is added.
The phase of development focused on understanding program constraints and the concerns of users. Methods include surveys, user testing, interviews, and direct observations.
A description of how a program functions, including user interactions it must provide. Requirements are used to identify appropriate inputs for testing.
A document that defines the requirements for a program. The design phase outlines how to accomplish the specification.

cards.front: What does the design phase of a program include?
cards.back: Brainstorming, planning and storyboarding, organizing into modules and functional components, creating UI layout diagrams, and developing a testing strategy.

A written description of the function of a code segment, event, procedure, or program and how it was developed. Should be written throughout development. Helps in developing and maintaining correct programs individually and collaboratively.
A form of program documentation written directly into the program to be read by people. Do not affect how the program runs. Not all programming environments support comments.
Any code developed collaboratively or borrowed from another source must be acknowledged. The acknowledgement goes in the program documentation and must include the origin or original author's name.
A mistake in the algorithm or program that causes it to behave incorrectly or unexpectedly. The program runs but produces wrong results.
A mistake in the program where the rules of the programming language are not followed. The program cannot run until the syntax error is fixed.
A mistake in the program that occurs during the execution of a program. Programming languages define their own run-time errors.
An error that occurs when a computer attempts to handle a number that is outside the defined range of values.

cards.front: Compare: Logic vs Syntax vs Run-Time Error
cards.back:
Logic error: program runs but produces wrong results.
Syntax error: program cannot run because language rules were broken.
Run-time error: program starts running but crashes or fails during execution.

cards.front: Ways to Find and Correct Errors
cards.back: Test cases, hand tracing, visualizations, debuggers, and adding extra output statements.
Using defined inputs to ensure an algorithm or program is producing expected outcomes. Results from testing are used to revise the algorithm or program.

cards.front: What inputs should be used when testing a program?
cards.back: Inputs that demonstrate different expected outcomes, including values at or just beyond the extremes (minimum and maximum) of the input data.
